# Mark 5: When Jesus Arrives, Everything Changes

When Jesus arrives, power breaks loose in ways that shatter what we thought was possible or permanent. The Gerasene demoniac has been shattered by forces beyond human control—chained, broken free, living among the dead, tormented by an internal army called Legion. His condition is not a riddle to be solved or a disease to be managed; it is utter bondage. Yet the moment he sees Jesus, something in him recognizes authority. The demon-possessed man runs toward Jesus, falls on his knees, and the unclean spirit inside him shouts in terror: "What do You want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God?" There is no ambiguity here. The spirits recognize what many in the crowd do not—they know exactly who Jesus is. He speaks one command, "Come out of this man," and the demons obey. Two thousand pigs rush into the sea, and the man sits clothed and in his right mind. The transformation is so absolute that the townspeople, faced with the evidence of Jesus's authority over the spiritual realm, do the unthinkable: they ask Him to leave. His power terrifies them more than the demon-possessed man ever did. They prefer their manageable misery to the disruption of an encounter with God.

That same unease follows Jesus across the water, where Jairus kneels before Him pleading for his dying daughter, and the crowd presses in with need and hunger. In this crush of desperation walks a woman who has suffered for twelve years—not possessed, but broken, depleted, abandoned by physicians and emptied of resources. She touches His garment in secret faith, believing that even contact with His clothing holds power to heal. Jesus immediately senses that power has gone out from Him. He stops. In a crowd surging with people, He searches for the one whose touch was different because her faith was different. When the woman trembles before Him and confesses, He calls her "Daughter" and tells her that her faith has healed her. Notice: He does not dismiss her need as small or her suffering as insignificant. He honors her trust and sends her into peace, fully free from her affliction.

But healing the woman has cost time, and messengers arrive with the worst news—Jairus's daughter is dead. Jesus overhears and turns to the grieving father with words that cut through all despair: "Do not be afraid; just believe." He enters the house where mourning crowds make noise and mockery, and He silences them. He takes the child's hand and speaks in Aramaic, "Talitha koum"—Little girl, get up—and she rises. Mark records no hesitation, no gradual awakening. There is only obedience and immediate restoration. Jesus then does something mundane: He tells her parents to give her food. Death has been reversed, but she is still a child who needs to eat. The divine and the ordinary belong together in His presence.

What Mark shows us across these three encounters is that Jesus does not negotiate with death, disease, or darkness. He commands them. Whether demons flee, bleeding stops, or the dead rise, it happens because He speaks and it is so. Yet what troubles us most is not His power but His claim on our obedience. The townspeople rejected Him because His presence demanded that they acknowledge a reality larger than their economic concerns or settled lives. We may say we want healing, yet when it comes, it often upends everything we thought we controlled. The real question Mark leaves with us is not whether Jesus has power—that is settled—but whether we will actually believe Him when He tells us: Do not be afraid. Just believe.
